meta.description: MVP test slice #5. Covers Free Exercise of Religion doctrine from Cantwell (1940) through Fulton (2021). Four-act structure: foundation/incorporation, compelling interest era (Sherbert/Yoder), Smith revolution (1990), post-Smith line (Lukumi/Fulton). Five schema issues surfaced (#24-28).

areas.label: Free Exercise of Religion › Doctrine
areas.description: The First Amendment's protection of religious belief and practice from government interference. Free Exercise doctrine distinguishes absolutely between belief — which government may never punish or compel — and religiously motivated conduct, which may be regulated subject to constitutional limits. The scope of those limits is the central contested question: from 1963 to 1990 the compelling interest test governed; from 1990 forward, Employment Division v. Smith held that neutral, generally applicable laws do not require compelling interest justification even when they substantially burden religious practice. The post-Smith framework remains formally intact but is actively contested within the Supreme Court.
areas.notes: status: disputed reflects the contested state of the Smith framework — formally good law but with at least four sitting justices having signaled willingness to overrule or significantly limit it. valid_from reflects Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940), which incorporated Free Exercise against the states. Historical background: Reynolds v. United States (1879) established the belief/conduct distinction before incorporation — belief absolutely protected, conduct regulable. Reynolds is treated as background history, not a full case node, because it predates incorporation and the modern constitutional framework.

doctrines.label: Free Exercise Doctrine
doctrines.description: The First Amendment absolutely protects religious belief — government may never punish, penalize, or coerce belief, or compel profession of any belief. Religiously motivated conduct receives substantial but not absolute protection. The threshold question is whether the law burdening religious conduct is neutral and generally applicable: if not, it triggers strict scrutiny regardless of Smith. If yes, it survives Free Exercise challenge without compelling interest justification under Smith. The belief/conduct distinction is foundational and not contested; the scope of conduct protection is the contested question.
elements: Religious belief is absolutely protected — government may never punish or compel belief
Religious conduct receives substantial but not absolute protection
Neutral, generally applicable laws that incidentally burden religious conduct do not require compelling interest justification (Smith rule)
Laws that are not neutral or not generally applicable trigger strict scrutiny regardless of Smith
Government may not selectively impose burdens on religious conduct that it does not impose on comparable secular conduct
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE A: The belief/conduct distinction functions as a threshold classification framework within Free Exercise doctrine — identical structurally to the public figure taxonomy in press freedom doctrine (Issue #19). The schema has no way to represent this as a structured pre-test classification. The distinction is not a prong of the Free Exercise test but a gateway that determines whether the test applies at all. Proposed fix: same as Issue #19 — PRECONDITION_TO edge type or structured sub-schema for threshold classifications. SCHEMA ISSUE C: The Smith rule is formally good law but the current Court has signaled in Fulton (2021) and in individual writings by at least four justices that Smith may be reconsidered. The status: disputed flag captures this but does not distinguish between (a) circuit split on application, (b) academic dispute, and (c) intra-Court challenge to a precedent by sitting justices. These are meaningfully different kinds of dispute for a research tool. Proposed fix: add a dispute_source enum to the status field: circuit_split | academic_consensus | intra_court | legislative_response.

doctrines.label: Belief-Conduct Distinction
doctrines.description: The foundational Free Exercise distinction between religious belief and religiously motivated conduct. Belief is absolutely protected: the government may never punish a person for holding a religious belief, compel a person to profess a belief, or penalize a person for changing their religion. Conduct, even when religiously motivated, is subject to government regulation — the degree of protection depends on whether the law is neutral and generally applicable (Smith) or not (Lukumi). The distinction was first articulated in Reynolds v. United States (1879) and incorporated into the modern constitutional framework by Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940).
elements: Government may never punish religious belief as such
Government may never compel profession or abandonment of religious belief
Government may never penalize a person for changing their religion
Religiously motivated conduct is regulable subject to neutrality and general applicability requirements
The belief/conduct line is determined by whether the government is targeting the belief itself or regulating conduct that happens to be religiously motivated

pending_establishment.note: Reynolds first articulated the belief/conduct distinction but predates incorporation and the modern Free Exercise framework. Treated as historical background. Full case node deferred.

doctrines.notes: The belief/conduct distinction is not contested — all justices across all eras agree on it. The dispute is entirely about how much protection conduct receives once the belief/conduct threshold is crossed.

doctrines.label: Neutrality and General Applicability Doctrine
doctrines.description: Under Employment Division v. Smith (1990) and Church of Lukumi (1993), the threshold question in any Free Exercise challenge to a law is whether the law is both neutral (not targeted at religious practice) and generally applicable (not riddled with exceptions for comparable secular conduct). If a law fails either requirement, strict scrutiny applies regardless of Smith's general rule. A law is not neutral if its object is to infringe upon or restrict religious practice. A law is not generally applicable if it exempts comparable secular conduct from its prohibitions while refusing religious exemptions, or if it creates a system of individualized exemptions that allow government discretion to grant or deny exemptions based on the nature of the reason for the exemption request.
elements: A law must be neutral: its object must not be to target or restrict religious practice
A law must be generally applicable: it must not selectively burden religious conduct while exempting comparable secular conduct
A law is not generally applicable if it creates a system of individualized exemptions that allow for discretionary secular exemptions but not religious ones (Fulton rule)
Lack of neutrality or general applicability triggers strict scrutiny regardless of Smith's general rule
The neutrality and general applicability inquiries are distinct: a law may fail either or both
doctrines.notes: valid_from reflects Lukumi (1993), which gave the neutrality/general applicability framework its definitive statement. The framework is implicit in Smith (1990) but not fully articulated there. Fulton (2021) significantly expanded the general applicability inquiry — a categorical exception in a law's own terms that allows for discretionary secular exemptions makes the law not generally applicable. SCHEMA ISSUE D: The determination of whether a law is neutral and generally applicable is a totality-of-circumstances analysis drawing on multiple factors without clean numbered prongs. Same structural problem as the opinion-fact distinction (Issue #23). Currently modeled as Doctrine. test_form: totality_of_circumstances would be the appropriate classification if promoted to DoctrinalTest.

doctrinal_tests.label: Sherbert Compelling Interest Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The pre-Smith constitutional standard for Free Exercise challenges. The government must satisfy strict scrutiny to enforce a law that substantially burdens religious practice: a compelling governmental interest pursued through the least restrictive means available. Established in Sherbert v. Verner (1963), extended in Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972). Superseded by Employment Division v. Smith (1990) for neutral, generally applicable laws, but remains operative for non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws and has been restored by statute (RFRA) for federal laws.

prongs.label: Substantial burden on religious practice
prongs.description: The law or government action must substantially burden the plaintiff's sincerely held religious belief or practice. A de minimis burden does not trigger the test. Courts do not evaluate the reasonableness or centrality of the belief — only its sincerity and the burden's substantiality.
prongs.burden_note: Plaintiff establishes prima facie case by showing substantial burden on sincere religious practice.
prongs.contest_note: What constitutes a 'substantial burden' is contested. Some circuits require that the government directly compel action contrary to religious belief; others find substantial burden where government conditions a significant benefit on conduct that violates religious belief (the Sherbert-Hobson's-choice pattern). The RFRA substantial burden standard has also generated significant circuit disagreement.

prongs.label: Compelling governmental interest
prongs.description: The government must demonstrate a compelling interest in enforcing the law against the religious objector. A general interest in uniform enforcement is insufficient — the government must show a particularized compelling interest in denying the specific exemption sought.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of demonstrating compelling interest. The interest must be compelling as applied to the claimant, not just compelling in the abstract.

prongs.label: Least restrictive means
prongs.description: The government must pursue the compelling interest through the least restrictive means available. If a less burdensome alternative would achieve the same compelling interest, the government must use it.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of demonstrating no less restrictive alternative is available.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Status: modified rather than overruled. Smith did not overrule Sherbert across the board — it limited Sherbert to non-neutral, non-generally-applicable laws. Sherbert remains operative in its original domain (unemployment compensation cases and analogous individualized exemption systems) and was restored by RFRA for federal laws. The Sherbert test is also the model for RFRA's statutory compelling interest test. SCHEMA ISSUE B: RFRA (42 U.S.C. § 2000bb) restored the Sherbert test as a statutory matter after Smith abandoned it constitutionally. The graph models the constitutional Sherbert test but cannot represent that a statute has restored an abandoned constitutional standard. The constitutional test and the statutory test now coexist and are applied in different contexts. Phase 2 statute layer problem.

doctrinal_tests.label: Smith Neutral General Law Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The post-1990 constitutional standard for Free Exercise challenges to neutral, generally applicable laws. Under Employment Division v. Smith, the Free Amendment does not relieve an individual of the obligation to comply with a valid and neutral law of general applicability on the ground that the law proscribes conduct that his religion prescribes. A neutral, generally applicable law that incidentally burdens religious practice does not require compelling interest justification — it need only satisfy rational basis review. The threshold question is whether the law is in fact neutral and generally applicable.

prongs.label: Law is neutral
prongs.description: The law's object must not be to target, restrict, or burden religious practice. A law is not neutral if it singles out religious conduct for special burdens, if it exempts comparable secular conduct while burdening religious conduct, or if its purpose (as revealed by text, history, or enforcement) is to disadvantage a particular religion or religion generally.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger bears initial burden of demonstrating the law is not neutral. If challenger establishes non-neutrality, burden shifts to government to satisfy strict scrutiny.
prongs.contest_note: The neutrality inquiry is contested at the margins. How much anti-religious legislative history is required to make a facially neutral law non-neutral? When does selective enforcement evidence non-neutrality? These questions are resolved inconsistently across circuits. Masterpiece Cakeshop v. Colorado Civil Rights Commission (2018) found non-neutrality based on commissioners' hostile statements — but the scope of that holding is disputed.

prongs.label: Law is generally applicable
prongs.description: The law must apply across the board without carving out exceptions for comparable secular conduct. A law is not generally applicable if it creates a system of individualized exemptions that allows secular reasons to excuse compliance but not religious ones, or if it exempts some comparable secular conduct while burdening religious conduct. Fulton (2021) significantly tightened this prong: even a categorical exception that permits discretionary secular exemptions makes the law not generally applicable.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate the law is not generally applicable by showing comparable secular conduct is exempted. The comparison class — what counts as 'comparable' — is a contested question.
prongs.contest_note: The general applicability inquiry is the most actively litigated aspect of post-Smith doctrine. What counts as a 'comparable' secular exemption? How many secular exceptions are needed before a law loses general applicability? Fulton held that a single discretionary exemption for secular reasons suffices to make a law not generally applicable — but the scope of Fulton's rule is being actively worked out in lower courts. Multiple circuits have disagreed on application.

prongs.label: Rational basis if neutral and generally applicable
prongs.description: If the law is both neutral and generally applicable, it need only satisfy rational basis review to survive Free Exercise challenge — the government need not demonstrate a compelling interest or least restrictive means. The religious objector has no constitutional right to an exemption from a valid neutral general law.
prongs.burden_note: If the law is neutral and generally applicable, the government need only show a rational basis. Burden effectively shifts to challenger to show the law lacks even a rational basis, which is nearly impossible to establish.
prongs.contest_note: The rational basis tier for neutral general laws is contested at the Supreme Court level — not in application but in validity. At least four sitting justices (Alito, Thomas, Gorsuch, and Barrett in Fulton) have questioned whether Smith correctly held that neutral general laws only require rational basis. If Smith is overruled, this prong disappears entirely and Sherbert's compelling interest standard is restored. SCHEMA ISSUE C: This is the first instance in the graph of a prong that is contested not because of application disagreements but because the test itself may be overruled. The contested flag was designed for circuit splits on prong meaning — not for intra-Court challenges to the test's existence.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Smith is the most contested Free Exercise precedent. Scalia's majority opinion was joined by only four other justices; O'Connor, joined by Brennan, Marshall, and Blackmun, concurred in the judgment but rejected the majority's reasoning and argued Sherbert should have been applied. The 5-4 split on reasoning (though not on result) foreshadowed the precedent's contested status. Justice Alito has written extensively against Smith in Fulton concurrences and other writings. The Barrett-Kavanaugh-Thomas concurrence in Fulton explicitly invited reconsideration.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Free Exercise Clause of the First Amendment is incorporated against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause. States may not punish religious solicitation or peaceful proselytizing. The Free Exercise Clause absolutely protects religious belief and protects religious conduct from arbitrary governmental interference, though conduct remains subject to reasonable regulation.
cases.notes: The foundational incorporation case for Free Exercise. Cantwell was a Jehovah's Witness convicted under a Connecticut statute requiring a license for religious solicitation and for breach of peace after playing anti-Catholic recordings on a Catholic street. The unanimous Court held the solicitation statute was an unconstitutional prior restraint and the breach of peace conviction was unconstitutional. Cantwell is also connected to the prior restraint doctrine — the licensing scheme for religious solicitation is exactly the kind of prior restraint Near v. Minnesota (1931) condemned for the press.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may not deny unemployment compensation to a Seventh-day Adventist who refused to work on Saturdays for religious reasons without satisfying strict scrutiny. The government must demonstrate a compelling interest in denying the religious exemption and pursue it through the least restrictive means. Government conditions on significant benefits that effectively require a person to choose between their religion and the benefit are subject to strict scrutiny.

opinions.author: Harlan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Harlan argued the majority's rule required the state to provide religious exemptions from neutral unemployment compensation rules, effectively preferring religion over comparable secular claims — an Establishment Clause problem. This dissent foreshadows the Smith majority's concern about religion-specific exemptions.

cases.notes: Status: limited because Smith (1990) superseded Sherbert as the general rule for neutral generally applicable laws. Sherbert remains operative in unemployment compensation cases and analogous individualized exemption contexts — Smith explicitly preserved it there. Harlan's Sherbert dissent was effectively adopted by Smith's majority reasoning about the Establishment Clause risks of religion-specific exemptions.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The First Amendment's Free Exercise Clause bars Wisconsin from enforcing its compulsory school attendance law against Amish parents who refused to send their children to school beyond eighth grade. The Amish religious way of life, the parents' sincerely held beliefs, and the demonstrated harm to those beliefs from compulsory attendance combined to satisfy the compelling interest test. The state's interest in compulsory education did not justify the burden on Amish religious practice.

opinions.author: Douglas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Douglas argued the children's own religious views — not just the parents' — should be determinative. He would have remanded to determine the children's individual wishes. This raised questions about parental religious authority vs. children's autonomous rights that remain unresolved.

cases.notes: The high-water mark of the Sherbert compelling interest era. Yoder is often cited as the most religion-protective Free Exercise decision ever issued. Status: limited because Smith superseded the Sherbert framework as the general rule. Yoder's specific holding — that the Amish are exempt from compulsory schooling — is still good law as applied to the Amish, but the methodology (compelling interest balancing) was significantly curtailed by Smith.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Free Exercise Clause does not relieve an individual of the obligation to comply with a neutral law of general applicability on the ground that the law proscribes conduct that the individual's religion prescribes. Oregon's criminal prohibition of peyote use — as applied to deny unemployment compensation to Native American church members who used peyote sacramentally — does not violate the Free Exercise Clause because the prohibition is a neutral, generally applicable criminal law.

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: O'Connor agreed the Free Exercise Clause did not protect the peyote use but argued the majority's reasoning was wrong — the Sherbert compelling interest test should have been applied and the state's interest in drug prohibition was compelling enough to override the religious exemption claim. O'Connor's concurrence represents the view that Smith misread Free Exercise doctrine and that Sherbert should remain the governing standard.

opinions.author: Blackmun
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Blackmun dissented on the merits — arguing Oregon's interest was not compelling and the sacramental peyote use should be protected. His dissent argues for the application of Sherbert to reach a different result, not just a different methodology.

cases.notes: Smith is formally good law but the most contested Free Exercise precedent. The 5-4 split on reasoning (not just result) — Scalia joined by Rehnquist, White, Stevens, Kennedy; O'Connor concurring in judgment only — foreshadowed the decision's contested status. Congress responded with RFRA (1993), attempting to restore Sherbert by statute. The Court struck down RFRA as applied to states in City of Boerne v. Flores (1997) but it remains operative for federal law. In Fulton (2021), Barrett, Kavanaugh, and Thomas explicitly invited reconsideration of Smith; Alito wrote separately at length criticizing it. SCHEMA ISSUE C: status: disputed is correct but insufficient — the dispute is an intra-Court challenge by sitting justices to the validity of a majority precedent, which is categorically different from a circuit split or academic dispute. The schema's dispute_source enum proposed in Issue #24 would handle this.

cases.full_name: Church of Lukumi Babalu Aye, Inc. v. City of Hialeah
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Hialeah ordinances targeting Santeria animal sacrifice are neither neutral nor generally applicable and therefore cannot survive strict scrutiny. A law fails neutrality if its object is to infringe upon or restrict religious practice. A law fails general applicability if it prohibits religiously motivated conduct but permits comparable secular conduct. The ordinances here were gerrymandered to target Santeria while exempting comparable animal killing for secular purposes.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Scalia concurred separately to emphasize that the neutrality and general applicability framework flows directly from Smith — Lukumi is not an exception to Smith but an application of it.

cases.notes: Lukumi is the essential companion to Smith. Smith said neutral general laws don't require compelling interest; Lukumi said non-neutral non-general laws do. The unanimous judgment (with separate reasoning from various justices) gave the neutrality and general applicability framework its definitive statement. The Hialeah ordinances were so obviously targeted at Santeria that they provided an easy case; the harder questions about what counts as non-neutral or non-generally-applicable are still being worked out.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Philadelphia's refusal to contract with Catholic Social Services for foster care placement because CSS would not certify same-sex couples violated the Free Exercise Clause. Philadelphia's standard foster care contract included a provision allowing the Commissioner to grant exceptions to the non-discrimination requirement. This discretionary exception authority made the non-discrimination requirement not generally applicable, triggering strict scrutiny that Philadelphia could not satisfy.

opinions.author: Barrett
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Barrett's concurrence explicitly invited reconsideration of Smith, arguing the textual and historical case for Smith's rule is not as strong as Scalia claimed, and that the Court should take up a case presenting the Smith question directly. This concurrence signals that at least three justices are ready to overrule Smith.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Alito wrote at length that Smith was wrongly decided and should be overruled, offering a comprehensive critique of Smith's historical foundation, textual basis, and practical consequences. Joined by Thomas and Gorsuch.

opinions.author: Gorsuch
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Gorsuch argued the majority's reading of general applicability was too narrow and that Smith should be reconsidered directly.

cases.notes: Fulton is decided 9-0 on the result but with profound disagreement on the rationale. Roberts's majority decided on the narrowest possible grounds — the Commissioner's discretionary exception authority made the policy not generally applicable — avoiding the Smith question entirely. Six justices wrote separately to criticize Smith. The Barrett (Kavanaugh only — Thomas joined Alito-Gorsuch separately), Alito (Thomas, Gorsuch), and Gorsuch (Thomas, Alito) concurrences together represent at least four justices ready to overrule Smith (Thomas joins both Barrett and Alito-Gorsuch concurrences). Roberts and the two newest justices (Jackson, who replaced Breyer after Fulton) have not signaled their views on Smith. The stage is set for Smith's reconsideration in a future case.

edges.notes: The Free Exercise Clause is textually distinct from the Speech and Press Clauses — 'or prohibiting the free exercise thereof' — and provides the constitutional grounding for this area.

condition.context: Applies to non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws, and to individualized exemption systems
condition.note: Sherbert remains operative where Smith does not apply — non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws, unemployment compensation cases, and is restored by RFRA for federal laws

condition.context: Applies only to neutral, generally applicable laws
condition.note: Smith test governs neutral general laws only. Non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws trigger strict scrutiny regardless of Smith.

edges.notes: RELATED_TO criteria: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940) involved a licensing scheme for religious solicitation that is simultaneously a prior restraint case and a Free Exercise case. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — prior restraint governs the mechanism of pre-publication suppression; free exercise governs government targeting of religious practice. Different analytical frameworks protecting related First Amendment interests.
Cantwell incorporated the Free Exercise Clause against states via the 14th Amendment Due Process Clause.

Expands: Sherbert significantly expanded the protection of religiously motivated conduct by requiring the government to satisfy strict scrutiny before enforcing laws that substantially burden religious practice — even neutral generally applicable laws.

Expands: Yoder extended the Sherbert compelling interest framework to compulsory education laws and established that the government's interest in compulsory schooling was not compelling enough to override deeply held religious objections backed by a demonstrated religious way of life. The case set the high-water mark for Free Exercise protection.

Narrows: Smith significantly narrowed Sherbert by holding that the compelling interest test applies only to non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws — not to neutral general laws that incidentally burden religious practice. This is not a full overruling of Sherbert (which remains operative in unemployment compensation cases and analogous contexts) but a major narrowing of its scope.
Narrows: Smith significantly narrowed the scope of Free Exercise protection for religiously motivated conduct by holding that neutral general laws require only rational basis review.

Harlan's Sherbert dissent (1963) argued that religion-specific exemptions from neutral laws raise Establishment Clause concerns — the precise reasoning Scalia's Smith majority adopted 27 years later. This is a direct intellectual lineage from a dissent to a majority holding.

Clarifies: Lukumi gave definitive content to what Smith meant by neutral and generally applicable — standards that Smith articulated but did not fully define. This is a classic clarification: it resolves ambiguity in the test without narrowing or expanding its scope.

Clarifies: Fulton significantly expanded the general applicability inquiry by holding that a categorical exception in a contract that allows for discretionary secular exemptions makes the entire policy not generally applicable — even if no such exemption had ever been granted. This is a clarification that in practice significantly expands when strict scrutiny applies, but without formally changing the Smith framework.

Expands: Fulton expanded the general applicability doctrine by holding that a mere discretionary exception authority — even unused — suffices to make a law not generally applicable. This significantly expands the category of laws that trigger strict scrutiny.

schema_issues_found.location: free_exercise_doctrine (Doctrine node) / employment_division_v_smith_1990 (Case node) / smith_test (DoctrinalTest node)
schema_issues_found.description: Intra-Court challenge to a precedent — dispute_source needed. The Smith rule is formally good law but at least four sitting justices have explicitly called for its reconsideration (Barrett, Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch in Fulton concurrences). The status: disputed flag is correct but conflates four distinct types of doctrinal instability: (1) circuit split on application, (2) academic consensus against the rule, (3) intra-Court challenge by sitting justices, and (4) legislative response (RFRA). These are meaningfully different for a research tool — a circuit split means lower courts are divided; an intra-Court challenge means the Supreme Court may revisit the rule at any time. Proposed fix: add dispute_source enum to status fields: circuit_split | academic | intra_court_challenge | legislative_response | post_overruling_transition. Multiple values should be permitted.

Threshold classification framework — second instance of Issue #19 meta-test problem. The belief/conduct distinction is a precondition to Free Exercise analysis, not a test applied to the merits. It determines whether the conduct is even subject to constitutional protection analysis. Like the public figure taxonomy (Issue #19), it is a gateway classification, not a merits test. The GOVERNED_BY edge is accurate but cannot represent that belief_conduct_distinction is a precondition to both sherbert_test and smith_test. The PRECONDITION_TO edge type proposed in Issue #19 would handle this. This second instance confirms the pattern is recurring and the fix is needed before Equal Protection (where tiered scrutiny is the same kind of threshold classification).

Constitutional test restored by statute — schema cannot represent this. The Sherbert compelling interest test was significantly curtailed by Smith (1990) as a constitutional matter, but Congress responded with RFRA (1993), which restored the Sherbert test as a statutory standard for federal laws. City of Boerne v. Flores (1997) struck down RFRA as applied to state laws. The graph models the constitutional Sherbert test. It cannot represent that: (1) the constitutional test was narrowed, (2) a statute restored the narrowed test for federal laws, and (3) that statute was partially invalidated, leaving the test operative only for federal laws. This is a three-layer constitutional-statutory interaction that requires the Phase 2 statute layer to model completely. Proposed interim fix: add a statutory_restoration note field to DoctrinalTest nodes documenting when a statute has restored a limited constitutional test.

Totality-of-circumstances analysis without clean prongs — second instance of Issue #23. The determination of whether a law is neutral and generally applicable draws on multiple factors (text, history, enforcement patterns, structure of exceptions) that courts apply holistically rather than sequentially. This is the same structural problem as the opinion-fact distinction (Issue #23). The neutrality_general_applicability_doctrine is modeled as a Doctrine rather than a DoctrinalTest because its factors lack the sequential burden structure of a formal test. The test_form field proposed in Issue #23 (totality_of_circumstances) would apply here. This second instance confirms the pattern is sufficiently common to warrant the field addition in v0.5.

Conditional GOVERNED_BY edges with overlapping validity windows — temporal ambiguity. Both the Sherbert test and the Smith test GOVERN the free_exercise Area simultaneously, with overlapping valid_from dates and no valid_until on either. This is accurate — both tests remain operative but in different circumstances — but creates graph ambiguity: a query for 'what test currently governs free exercise?' returns both tests without indicating that Smith governs neutral general laws and Sherbert governs everything else. The condition attribute on GOVERNED_BY edges (added in v0.4 for instrument-type conditionality) is used here to document the conditionality, but the condition attribute is optional and not queryable as a structured field. Proposed fix: promote condition.context to a structured enum rather than a free string, so queries can filter GOVERNED_BY edges by governing condition.
